title: In Hospital - II - Waiting
content:
A square, squat room (a cellar on promotion),
    Drab to the soul, drab to the very daylight;
    Plasters astray in unnatural-looking tinware;
    Scissors and lint and apothecary's jars.

    Here, on a bench a skeleton would writhe from,
    Angry and sore, I wait to be admitted:
    Wait till my heart is lead upon my stomach,
    While at their ease two dressers do their chores.

    One has a probe - it feels to me a crowbar.
    A small boy sniffs and shudders after bluestone.
    A poor old tramp explains his poor old ulcers.
    Life is (I think) a blunder and a shame.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, William Ernest Henley delivers a powerful performance in "In Hospital - II - Waiting"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
